description: The provided image is an unrelated software interface element displaying the text 'Check for updates', which has no association with the research content of the attached paper focusing on Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel. No valid information related to the material's microstructure, mechanical properties, heat treatment processes, or characterization results can be extracted from this mismatched image. The associated paper aims to improve the impact toughness of the studied low-density steel via multi-stage heat treatment while maintaining high strength and ductility, but no relevant figure content corresponding to this research is visible in the provided image.

This figure is the flow chart of hot rolling and heat treatment processes for Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel. The common pre-processing for all samples includes: homogenization of the ingot at 1200 °C for 2 h, followed by hot rolling at 1050–950 °C to 6 mm thick sheet, then air cooling to room temperature. Two heat treatment routes are set: ① Single-stage heat treatment: samples are held at 1000 °C, 1050 °C, 1100 °C respectively for 3 min then quenched to room temperature, denoted as S1000, S1050, S1100 (S1100 is only used for tensile property comparison); ② Multi-stage heat treatment (MHT): three successive steps, first holding at 1000 °C for 3 min and quenching, then holding at 750 °C for 15 min and quenching, finally holding at 1050 °C for 3 min and quenching. This schematic clarifies the parameter differences of different heat treatment processes, providing a process basis for subsequent microstructure and property comparison studies.

This figure consists of 6 subgraphs characterizing Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel subjected to three heat treatments: S1000 (single-stage 1000 °C treatment), S1050 (single-stage 1050 °C treatment), and MHT (multi-stage heat treatment). Subgraphs (a-c) are cross-sectional SEM micrographs (20 μm scale bar) showing microstructure: (a) exhibits austenite grains, twins, and grain boundary precipitated Cr₇C₃, while (b, c) show twins without obvious intergranular Cr₇C₃. Subgraphs (d-f) are EBSD grain boundary maps and misorientation angle distribution plots: black lines represent random high angle grain boundaries, red lines represent Σ≤29 special grain boundaries (RHABs), with scale bars of 10 μm for (d,e) and 20 μm for (f). The misorientation distributions show a dominant peak near 60°, corresponding to Σ3 annealing twins. Statistically, special grain boundary fractions are 71.7% for S1000, 68.3% for S1050, and 71.2% for MHT, with MHT having more but smaller twins due to increased nucleation sites from intermediate 750 °C treatment precipitates. This figure demonstrates that multi-stage heat treatment regulates grain boundary features to support toughness improvement without strength and ductility loss.

This figure presents TEM micrographs and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns of precipitates in Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel subjected to three heat treatment conditions: S1000 (1000 °C single-stage solution treatment, subfigures a-d), S1050 (1050 °C single-stage solution treatment, subfigures e-h), and MHT (multi-stage heat treatment, subfigures i-l). Dark-field TEM images (a, e, i) mark 3–10 nm nanoscale κ-carbide particles coherent with austenite with white arrows, and corresponding SAED patterns (b, f, j) confirm the orientation relationship between austenite (γ) and κ-carbide: [011]γ//[011]κ. For S1000, bright-field TEM (c) shows elongated stripe-like Cr₇C₃ precipitates along austenite grain boundaries, with SAED (d) indexing Cr₇C₃ at [112] zone axis. For S1050, bright-field TEM (g) shows 20–160 nm B2 phase particles within grains, indexed by SAED (h) at [111] B2 zone axis. For MHT, bright-field TEM (k) shows B2 phase with slightly smaller size than that in S1050, indexed by SAED (l) at [113] B2 zone axis. The results reveal that grain boundary Cr₇C₃ dissolves at higher solution temperature, and multi-stage heat treatment modulates precipitate characteristics to optimize grain boundary properties, providing microstructural evidence for the impact toughness improvement of the steel without compromising strength and ductility.

This figure presents tensile mechanical properties of Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel treated by four different heat treatment processes: multi-stage heat treatment (MHT), single-stage solution treatment at 1000 °C (S1000), 1050 °C (S1050), and 1100 °C (S1100). Subplot (a) shows engineering stress-strain curves, with engineering strain in % as x-axis and engineering stress in MPa as y-axis. Subplot (b) displays true stress-strain curves (solid lines) and strain work hardening rates (dotted lines), with true strain as x-axis and true stress/strain hardening rate in MPa as y-axis. Yield strength and ultimate tensile strength decrease with increasing solution treatment temperature: S1000 has the highest strength, followed by S1050, MHT, and S1100, while S1000, S1050 and MHT all retain total elongation above 50%. Strain hardening rates of all samples show three typical stages, with a peak around true strain of 0.2, indicating excellent work hardening capability. This figure confirms that MHT maintains favorable strength-ductility matching, supporting its good comprehensive mechanical performance.

This figure presents the impact mechanical properties of Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel treated by three different heat treatment routes: S1000 (single-stage solution treatment at 1000 °C followed by quenching), S1050 (single-stage solution treatment at 1050 °C followed by quenching), and MHT (multi-stage heat treatment). Subplot (a) shows load-displacement curves from instrumented Charpy impact tests, with x-axis as displacement (unit: mm) and y-axis as loading (unit: N); the inset displays the dimension of the Charpy impact sample (55 mm × 10 mm × 5 mm, RD refers to rolling direction). The curves indicate that MHT has the largest fracture displacement (~8.04 mm), followed by S1050 (~6.30 mm) and S1000 (~4.55 mm). Subplot (b) is a stacked bar chart illustrating crack initiation energy and crack propagation energy for the three samples. The total impact energy is 29.2 J for S1000, 42.8 J for S1050, and the highest 55.1 J for MHT. The improvement of impact toughness is mainly attributed to the increase of crack propagation energy, which rises from 9.87 J for S1000 to 36.78 J for MHT, confirming that multi-stage heat treatment effectively improves the impact toughness of the studied low-density steel via grain boundary optimization without compromising strength and ductility.

This figure presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) tensile fracture morphologies of Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel subjected to different heat treatments. Subfigure (a) corresponds to sample S1000 (single-stage heat treatment at 1000 °C for 3 min followed by quenching), (b) corresponds to sample S1050 (single-stage heat treatment at 1050 °C for 3 min followed by quenching), (c) corresponds to sample MHT (multi-stage heat treatment). Each subfigure includes a high-magnification fracture micrograph with a 10 μm scale bar, and a low-magnification macrograph of the fractured tensile sample (0.5 mm scale bar) in the yellow bordered inset. Dimple features, characteristic of ductile fracture, are observed on the fracture surfaces of all three samples, consistent with their high tensile elongation (50.9% to 55.8%). These results confirm that all three treated samples exhibit ductile tensile fracture, and the multi-stage heat treatment can maintain excellent ductility while improving the impact toughness of the studied low-density steel without obvious sacrifice of strength.

This group of SEM images presents the impact fracture morphologies of Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel subjected to different heat treatments. Subfigure (a) shows the fracture of sample S1000 (single-stage heat treatment at 1000 °C for 3 min followed by quenching), which features quasi-cleavage facets, microcracks, and microvoids, indicating mixed brittle-ductile fracture. Subfigure (b) corresponds to sample S1050 (single-stage heat treatment at 1050 °C for 3 min followed by quenching), also presenting quasi-cleavage, microcracks and microvoids with weaker brittle characteristics than S1000. Subfigure (c) shows the fracture of sample MHT (multi-stage heat treatment), which is fully covered by dimples, demonstrating typical ductile fracture. Low-magnification macroscopic morphologies of the impact test specimens are attached in the yellow-bordered inset for each subfigure. The fracture evolution confirms that multi-stage heat treatment eliminates harmful intergranular Cr7C3 precipitates and optimizes grain boundaries, greatly improving the impact toughness of the steel without sacrificing strength and ductility, consistent with the measured impact energy of 55.1 J for MHT, 42.8 J for S1050 and 29.2 J for S1000.

This set of cross-sectional SEM micrographs shows crack propagation regions of Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel subjected to different heat treatments. Subgraphs (a, b) are for sample S1000 (single-stage 1000 °C heat treatment), where Cr₇C₃ precipitates at grain boundaries cause preferential intergranular crack nucleation and propagation. Subgraphs (c, d) are for sample S1050 (single-stage 1050 °C heat treatment), where intergranular precipitates are eliminated, and cracks show deflection and trans-twin propagation with relatively straight paths. Subgraphs (e, f) are for MHT sample (multi-stage heat treatment), which has higher special grain boundary fraction and larger grain size, leading to zigzag crack paths with frequent deflection and trans-twin propagation, increasing effective crack propagation length and energy dissipation. The figure verifies that MHT improves impact toughness by 29% compared with S1000 via optimizing grain boundary features while maintaining good strength and ductility.

This set of SEM micrographs shows the cross-sectional microstructure of Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel after the 750 °C intermediate heat treatment step in the multi-stage heat treatment (MHT) process. Subplot (a) with a 10 μm scale bar presents the general microstructure, including austenite grains and clear grain boundary networks, with the dashed box marking the region magnified in subplot (b). Subplot (b) with a 2 μm scale bar shows the high-magnification morphology of the selected grain boundary region, where a large number of fine precipitates are observed distributing along grain boundaries. These closely spaced precipitates increase the stress in non-twinned regions, provide more twin nucleation sites, raise the proportion of special grain boundaries to interfere with random high-angle grain boundaries, and finally improve the impact toughness of the steel without sacrificing its high strength and ductility, supporting the core conclusion of MHT optimization for grain boundary structure.

This figure contains two schematic diagrams illustrating crack propagation behaviors in Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel subjected to different heat treatments. Subfigure (a) corresponds to sample S1000 treated with single-stage heat treatment at 1000 °C: Cr₇C₃ precipitates exist at austenite grain boundaries, cracks propagate rapidly along high-energy random grain boundaries, with relatively short propagation path and low energy consumption. Subfigure (b) corresponds to sample MHT treated with multi-stage heat treatment: grain boundary Cr₇C₃ precipitates are eliminated, with increased grain size and higher proportion of low-energy special grain boundaries (Σ boundaries). Cracks are hindered and deflected by these special boundaries during propagation, forming longer and more tortuous paths that consume more energy. The figure demonstrates that grain boundary optimization via multi-stage heat treatment improves impact toughness of the steel by increasing crack propagation energy consumption, without compromising strength and ductility significantly.
